perturbation.original_text: Recipient acknowledges that the United States securities laws prohibit it or any person who has received material nonpublic information about the Owner from purchasing or selling securities of the Owner or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance on such information, and agrees not to purchase, sell or otherwise engage in transactions in Owner’s stock based on such material nonpublic information until such time as the information becomes disseminated to the public either through a press release issued by Owner or through a filing made by Owner pursuant to the Securities CONFIDENTIAL NDA-CSC Standard Page 1 of 3 Exchange Act of 1934, as amended; provided, however, that Recipient may make any disclosure or use of such information to which Owner gives its prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges that the United States securities laws may apply to material nonpublic information about the Owner. Recipient agrees to consider the applicability of such laws. Recipient may make any disclosure or use of such information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly prohibits insider trading based on material non-public information, referencing the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The modified text weakens this prohibition by using vague language ('may apply,' 'consider the applicability') and removing the explicit prohibition on using the information for trading. It also removes the stipulation that any disclosure must be with prior written consent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5, which prohibit the use of any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. This includes trading on the basis of material nonpublic information, which is the core issue addressed in the original text. The modified text's vagueness undermines the legal prohibition against insider trading.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The original text contained an explicit prohibition on insider trading, referencing the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The changed text weakens this by using vague language, such as 'may apply' and 'consider the applicability.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, states that it is unlawful for any person to 'employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud' or 'engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security.' The modified text undermines this prohibition by only requiring the recipient to 'consider the applicability' of securities laws, rather than explicitly prohibiting insider trading based on material non-public information, thus creating a legal contradiction by omission.

perturbation.original_text: If Recipient is required to disclose Owner’s Confidential Information pursuant to a valid order by a court or other governmental body or as otherwise required by law, prior to any such compelled disclosure, Recipient will (i) notify Owner of the legal process, and allow Owner to assert the privileged and confidential nature of the Confidential Information against the third party seeking disclosure and (ii) reasonably cooperate with Owner in protecting against any such disclosure and/or obtaining a protective order narrowing the scope of such disclosure and/or use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: If Recipient is required to disclose Owner’s Confidential Information pursuant to an order by a court or other governmental body or as otherwise required by law, Recipient will disclose the Confidential Information to the extent necessary to legally comply with such compelled disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates that the Recipient notify the Owner *prior* to disclosing confidential information under legal compulsion, giving the Owner the opportunity to challenge the disclosure. The modified text removes the crucial prior notification requirement, depriving the Owner of the chance to protect their confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly violated, the modification undermines the spirit of Washington Civil Rule (CR) 26(c), which governs protective orders in discovery. By eliminating the requirement to notify the owner before disclosing confidential information, the modified text deprives the owner of the opportunity to seek a protective order under CR 26(c) to limit the scope of disclosure or prevent it altogether. This creates a legal risk, as the owner is now less able to protect its confidential information during legal proceedings in Washington.

perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the requirement for the Recipient to notify the Owner prior to disclosing confidential information under legal compulsion. This omission contradicts the legal principle of allowing the owner to protect their confidential information, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While the Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address the specific notification requirement, it confirms the existence of court rules and resources related to legal proceedings in Washington State, implying the importance of due process and the opportunity to respond to legal actions affecting one's interests. The removal of the notification requirement deprives the Owner of this opportunity, thereby creating a significant legal contradiction as described in the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Washington, without reference to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies Washington law as the governing law. The modification allows the Recipient to choose the governing law. This could allow the Recipient to select a jurisdiction with less protection for confidential information, thus weakening the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.108.030(1)(a) requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. While the law does not mandate Washington law to govern the agreement, selecting a jurisdiction with demonstrably weaker trade secret protection could be argued as a failure to make 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, particularly if Washington is the principal place of business for one party. This is because weakening the governing law provisions undermines the overall enforceability and protection of confidential information under Washington law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.030: Remedies for misappropriation—Damages. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.030 19.108.020 << 19.108.030 >> 19.108.040 PDF RCW 19.108.030 Remedies for misappropriation — Damages. (1) In addition to or in lieu of injunctive relief, a complainant may recover damages for the actual loss caused by misappropriation. A complainant also may recover for the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing damages for actual loss. (2) If wilful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (1). [ 1981 c 286 s 3 .]

perturbation.justification: The modification, by allowing the Recipient to choose the governing law, introduces a legal contradiction. RCW 19.108.030(1) addresses remedies for misappropriation and damages. The Law Explanation states that choosing a jurisdiction with weaker trade secret protection could be a failure to make 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, potentially undermining the protections under Washington law. While RCW 19.108.030 doesn't explicitly mandate Washington law, the ability to choose a jurisdiction with demonstrably weaker protections directly contradicts the intent to provide remedies for misappropriation, as detailed in the provided snippets. This makes the change a direct contradiction, because choosing another jurisdiction could undermine the ability to enforce the remedies outlined in the Washington law, thus failing to protect trade secrets reasonably as suggested.
